# Circular Design 101: Principles and Best Practices for Creating Sustainable Products

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/64ec2d5ecdbfa4e558b029a7/668cf1ef4c2d6b820b67434d_p-1-ibd-2023-circular-design.webp)

"Circular Design 101" introduces key principles and best practices for creating sustainable products. The post outlines five principles: design for longevity, repair, disassembly, recyclability, and biodegradability. It recommends choosing sustainable materials, minimizing material diversity, creating modular designs, using standardized components, and optimizing manufacturing processes. A case study of Evergreen Labs' Glassia Water demonstrates these principles in action. The article emphasizes collaboration, customer education, and iterative implementation, concluding with a look at future technologies in circular design and a call for sustainable product creation and consumption.

In today's world, where environmental concerns are at the forefront of global discussions, the concept of a circular economy has gained significant traction. At the heart of this innovative approach lies circular design â a methodology that aims to create products that are not only functional and aesthetically pleasing but also sustainable throughout their lifecycle. This blog post will explore the key principles and best practices of circular design, offering insights from our experience at Evergreen Labs.

a) **Design for longevity:** The first principle of circular design is creating products that stand the test of time. This involves using durable materials and robust construction methods to ensure products remain functional for as long as possible.

b) **Design for repair and maintenance:** Products should be easy to repair and maintain, extending their usable life. This principle encourages the use of standardized parts and accessible components.

c) **Design for disassembly and reassembly:** At the end of a product's life, it should be easy to take apart. This facilitates the reuse or recycling of individual components.

d) **Design for recyclability:** When a product can no longer be repaired or reused, its materials should be easily recyclable. This principle involves choosing materials that are compatible with existing recycling systems.

e) **Design for biodegradability:** For certain products, designing them to safely biodegrade at the end of their life cycle can be an environmentally friendly option.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/64ec2d5ecdbfa4e558b029a7/668cf3e083209acc83adac3d_AD_4nXdRQWt2pXGJ54_bL4VMS9CydTries_UP3BWVBpI8cuD8lx7mPTxU1BrQBG3c707dmg9_100WQd3iEmeD4g5MYb83IeCfUlLkIY7PMcFLU4ON7WMcP5e1J9wiBiKKBalTrkJWHpGyRqqfVa9XZl4a8lmMt5M.png)

a) **Choose sustainable materials:** Opt for materials that are renewable, recycled, or have a lower environmental impact. At Evergreen Labs, we've found that bamboo, recycled plastics, and organic textiles often make excellent choices.

b) **Minimize material diversity:** Using fewer types of materials in a single product makes it easier to recycle at the end of its life. This practice also often simplifies the manufacturing process.

c) **Create modular designs:** Modularity allows for easy replacement of parts, upgrades, and customization. This approach not only extends a product's life but can also enhance user satisfaction.

d) **Implement standardized components:** Using standard parts across product lines or even industries makes repair and recycling more straightforward.

e) **Optimize manufacturing processes:** Efficient production methods that minimize waste and energy use are crucial for truly sustainable products.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/64ec2d5ecdbfa4e558b029a7/668cf3dfcdd4b66fae59fd04_AD_4nXfkg9qk-tSt4ymXYSPbTta2GZdBuJb9eOhy_wp9fwxmmca41zaDi0p7TquOOO3YYqM_nvA0G5joh58UYHMubp4Bmljjwxp4NfbKiCr0Z9Pb1AFaYtDs2tkWF1G-DBqR1PA5LIlxNZ0RbfpeV3T0xC-z7X0.png)

At Evergreen Labs, we've put these principles into practice with our Glassia Water social enterprise. This pioneer in innovative, scalable water refill facilities is designed with circularity in mind from the ground up.

a) We chose glass as the primary material for bottles due to its durability, reusability and infinite recyclability. This packaging type also replace single-use plastic bottles having a direct environmental impact.

b) The refill facilities are constructed using standardized, easily replaceable parts, facilitating easy maintenance and repair. We've also partnered with local technicians to ensure our stations can be serviced quickly and efficiently, adding both longevity and community value to our system.

c) The modular nature of Glassia Water facilities allows us to adapt and upgrade them as technology improves, significantly extending the system's lifespan.

d) Local services and local products: By only servicing clients within a 50 kilometer radius we can ensure our business model remains feasible and has the minimum environmental footprint.

By designing a system that eliminates single-use plastic bottles altogether, Glassia Water demonstrates how circular design principles can be applied not just to products, but to entire service models. This approach has allowed us to create a solution that is not only environmentally friendly but also economically viable and socially beneficial.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/64ec2d5ecdbfa4e558b029a7/668cf3dfd7132130650dc36f_AD_4nXewHVE9Wiz5-uC7TvP0RYk2ab4u9f6v3sNmIVEj95R_86qS2HibU4mYJwLInUBOSxDJmbyOJbk8uwXNFAodwDuLZedqEwiVhYv3sT4mmK1NJQy93ELH-2SyFAc0sgM9Nt6mt7menqe3FFoJdXVt9V8oO0KX.jpeg)

**The Future of Circular Design:**

As technology advances, we're seeing exciting possibilities for circular design. 3D printing is enabling on-demand production of spare parts, while AI and IoT are helping to optimize product use and maintenance. At Evergreen Labs, we're exploring how these technologies can further enhance our circular design practices.

Circular design is more than just a trend â it's a necessary shift in how we approach product creation and consumption. By embracing these principles and best practices, we can create products that not only meet consumer needs but also contribute to a more sustainable future. Whether you're a designer, manufacturer, or consumer, you have a role to play in the circular economy. Let's work together to close the loop and create a more sustainable world.

Sustainability

Integrating the 7 stages of the product development cycle in the circular economy
=================================================================================

8 minute read ⏱

Last update: May 2025

The traditional linear economy model operates on a simple yet unsustainable framework: extract raw materials, produce goods, use them, and then dispose of them. This **product development life cycle** has dominated industrial practices for decades, resulting in an over-reliance on finite resources and contributing to significant environmental damage, from resource depletion to increasing landfills and pollution.

However, with growing concerns over this model’s environmental impact, many businesses are shifting toward a more sustainable alternative: the circular economy. The circular economy is designed to keep materials in circulation for as long as possible, minimizing waste and maximizing the use of existing resources.

By integrating circular economy principles into the product development life cycle, businesses can reduce their environmental footprint, use resources more efficiently, and contribute to long-term sustainability goals.

This article will explore how businesses can incorporate circular principles into product development, rethinking traditional processes to create more sustainable, future-proof solutions. We’ll begin by outlining the 7 stages of the product development cycle before discussing how each stage can be adapted to support circular economy goals, like reducing waste and extending product life.

1.  **[Understanding the circular economy](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#circular "Understanding the circular economy")**
2.  **[The product development life cycle in a circular economy](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#economy "The product development life cycle in a circular economy")**
3.  **[The 7 stages of the product development cycle](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#stages "The 7 stages of the product development cycle")**
4.  **[How to apply circular economy principles in the Product Development?](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#principles "How to apply circular economy principles in the Product Development?")**
5.  **[How to handle challenges in a circular product development life cycle?](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#challenges "How to handle challenges in a circular product development life cycle?")**
6.  **[The role of policymakers and industry in shaping a circular future](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#future "The role of policymakers and industry in shaping a circular future")**
7.  ************[A path towards sustainability in product development life cycle](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/product-development-cycle/#path "A path towards sustainability in product development life cycle")************

**************Understanding the circular economy**************
--------------------------------------------------------------

The circular economy aims to keep materials, components, and products in use for as long as possible, avoiding waste at every step. This model contrasts sharply with the linear economy, where products are typically discarded after use, leading to environmental damage and the depletion of natural resources.

The circular economy operates on three key principles:

1.  **Design out waste and pollution.** Products are designed for reuse and repair, not just for single use.
2.  **Keep products and materials in use.** This involves strategies such as repair, refurbishment, and remanufacturing.
3.  **Regenerate natural systems.** It also ensures materials and production processes contribute to environmental regeneration, not depletion.

The circular economy relies on waste management solutions that encourage the recycling and repurposing of materials. Organizations including the [Ellen MacArthur Foundation](https://www.ellenmacarthurfoundation.org/) have been crucial in championing these principles and pushing industries and governments, especially in Europe, toward adopting circular economy initiatives.

These efforts aim to reduce dependence on finite resources and build a sustainable future for businesses and society.

### Make a difference with IMD Sustainable business Programs 🌳

You know that sustainability is an imperative for your business. The real challenge is knowing how to transition from commitment to action, to successfully navigate the complexity of your sustainability transformation.

[Start your journey](https://www.imd.org/sustainability-programs/)

**************The product development life cycle in a circular economy**************
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The product development life cycle typically involves several key stages: design, manufacturing, use, and end-of-life disposal. Each of these stages presents an opportunity to incorporate circular economy principles.

In a circular model, the focus shifts from simply delivering a product to ensuring that its entire life cycle – from raw material sourcing to post-use disposal – is optimized for resource efficiency and sustainability.

*   **Design.** Products are created with longevity, repairability, and disassembly in mind.
*   **Manufacturing.** Processes prioritize the use of recycled materials and minimize waste.
*   **Use.** Products are durable and designed for multiple uses, encouraging consumers to repair rather than replace.
*   **End-of-life****.** Products are designed for easy recycling or remanufacturing, ensuring materials remain in circulation.

Effective **product life cycle management (PLM)** is essential for supporting these goals. PLM involves tracking a product’s entire life cycle and ensuring that each stage aligns with circular principles.

**Life cycle assessment (LCA)** tools help businesses evaluate their products’ environmental impact at every stage, making it easier to identify opportunities to reduce waste and improve sustainability. By incorporating these strategies, companies can shift from a linear model to a truly circular one.

************The 7 stages of the product development cycle************
---------------------------------------------------------------------

The product development cycle is a structured process that takes an idea from concept to market-ready product. Each stage plays a crucial role in ensuring the product is viable, functional, and **aligned with market demand**.

By incorporating circular principles into this cycle, businesses can create products that are not only innovative but also sustainable, reducing waste and extending product life.

Let’s explore each of the seven stages and how they contribute to the overall success of a product while supporting a more sustainable future.

### **1\. Ideation**

This stage is where creativity and innovation come together to generate ideas for a new product. Businesses gather input from various stakeholders, **brainstorm** concepts, and explore different solutions to existing problems or gaps in the market.

Circular principles can be introduced here by considering the product’s life cycle from the start, with an emphasis on using sustainable materials and designing for longevity and reuse.

### **2\. Research**

Research is critical for validating ideas and understanding market demand. During this stage, businesses gather data on customer needs, market trends, and competitor products. This information helps determine if the product idea is viable.

When applying circular principles, research should also include the [environmental impact](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/impact-investing/?ref=blog "environmental impact") of materials, production methods, and disposal options, ensuring the product aligns with sustainability goals.

### **3\. Planning**

The planning phase involves defining the product’s features, target market, and production process. A **project roadmap** is developed, outlining timelines, resources, and milestones.

Circular thinking here might include [strategic planning](https://www.imd.org/blog/strategy/strategic-planning-process/?ref=blog "strategic planning") for modularity, repairability, and the use of recycled or renewable materials, setting a foundation for a product that can be reused, refurbished, or responsibly recycled.

### **4\. Prototyping**

Prototyping brings ideas to life with tangible models or samples. This is where businesses test the product’s functionality, design, and feasibility. Multiple iterations are common during this stage.

To incorporate circular principles, prototypes should focus on minimizing material waste, improving efficiency, and designing for disassembly, making it easier to recycle or repurpose components later on.

### **5\. Sourcing**

During sourcing, businesses identify suppliers for the materials and components needed to manufacture the product. It’s essential to ensure that suppliers meet quality and cost requirements.

With circular principles in mind, businesses can prioritize [ethical suppliers](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/business-ethics/?ref=blog "ethical suppliers") who use sustainable practices, offer recycled or biodegradable materials, and support the reuse of products or packaging.

### **6\. Costing**

Costing involves calculating the total expenses involved in producing the product, including materials, labor, and overhead. Businesses need to ensure the product can be made at a price that aligns with customer expectations and business goals.

While circular designs might initially seem costlier, they often save money in the long run through resource efficiency, reduced waste, and extended product life.

### **7\. Commercialization**

Commercialization is the final stage, where the product is launched into the market. This involves production, marketing, distribution, and sales. Incorporating circular principles during commercialization means promoting the product’s sustainability benefits, ensuring that packaging is recyclable or reusable, and providing options for customers to return or recycle the product at the end of its life cycle.

![Image 2:  - IMD Business School](https://www.imd.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/20221128-MBA-SM-Live-Chat-event-sustainability-720x560-2x-PG-1.jpg)

************How to apply circular economy principles in the Product Development Life Cycle?************
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Incorporating circular economy principles into the Product Development Life Cycle involves rethinking traditional approaches to design, production, and end-of-life processes. By focusing on sustainability, resource efficiency, and waste reduction at each stage, businesses can create products that support long-term environmental goals while meeting consumer needs.

Here’s how to integrate circular thinking into every phase of product development.

### **Circular product design: Durability, repairability, and disassembly**

**Circular product design** is one of the most important aspects of the circular economy. The design phase is where decisions are made that determine how long a product will last, how easy it is to repair, and how it will be disposed of at the end of its life.

To align with circular principles, companies should focus on three main design factors:

1.  **Durability****.** Products should be built to last longer, reducing the need for frequent replacements. This not only saves resources but also enhances customer satisfaction.
2.  **Repairability.** Products designed with repair in mind can be easily fixed when something goes wrong, extending their useful life. For example, companies can reduce waste and encourage sustainable product design by making spare parts readily available and ensuring that products can be disassembled and reassembled easily.
3.  **Disassembly****.** Products should be designed for easy disassembly at the end of their life, allowing components to be recycled or reused in new products. This requires careful planning during the design phase to ensure that materials can be separated without contamination or damage.

By focusing on these design principles, businesses can create products that support remanufacturing, reduce the need for virgin materials, and minimize resource consumption.

### **Circular business models and supply chain transformation**

Transitioning to a circular economy also involves [rethinking traditional business models](https://www.imd.org/research-knowledge/strategy/articles/innovation-happens-elsewhere-rethinking-your-business-to-grow-and-compete/?ref=blog). The linear approach to business is based on continuous production and consumption, with little regard for waste. Circular business models, on the other hand, focus on keeping products and materials in circulation, reducing waste, and maximizing resource efficiency. Some common circular business models include:

*   **Product-as-a-service.** Instead of selling products outright, companies lease or rent them to consumers. This keeps products in use longer and allows them to be returned for refurbishment or remanufacturing.
*   **Leasing.** Similar to product-as-a-service, leasing allows consumers to use a product without owning it. The product can be returned to the manufacturer for reuse or recycling at the end of its life cycle.
*   **Remanufacturing****.** Products are returned to the manufacturer, who refurbishes or rebuilds them for resale, extending their useful life and reducing waste.

For these models to succeed, businesses must transform their [supply chains](https://www.imd.org/blog/management/supply-chain-management/?ref=blog) to support circular processes. This might involve sourcing sustainable materials, partnering with suppliers to reduce waste, and ensuring that closed-loop systems are in place to facilitate the reuse of materials. Adopting resource-efficient supply chains can help companies reduce their environmental impact and operational costs.

### **Circular initiatives in the product development process**

Implementing [circular economy principles](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/economic-sustainability/?ref=blog) into product development is not always straightforward. However, businesses can take several practical steps to get on the right path:

*   **Use** **recycled materials****.** Incorporating recycled materials into the manufacturing process reduces the need for virgin resources and helps close the loop on waste.
*   **Design for longevity.** Creating products that are durable and can be easily repaired or upgraded reduces the need for frequent replacements.
*   **Optimize** **energy consumption****.** Reducing energy consumption during the manufacturing process and using renewable energy sources helps minimize the environmental impact of production.
*   **Assess** **environmental impact****.** Conduct a thorough LCA to identify opportunities for reducing waste and emissions across the product’s entire life cycle.

These [**sustainable practices**](https://www.imd.org/research-knowledge/sustainability/articles/reaching-for-the-restorative-rethinking-the-sustainability-journey/?ref=blog) can help businesses gradually transition to a circular model, benefiting both the environment and their bottom line.

************How to handle challenges in a circular product development life cycle?************
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While the benefits of circular product development are clear, businesses face several challenges when making the transition:

*   **High upfront costs.** Redesigning products and reconfiguring supply chains to support circular processes can be expensive in the short term.
*   **Regulatory** **hurdles.** Navigating regulatory frameworks can be complicated, especially in industries with strict product safety standards.
*   **Stakeholder** **resistance.** Convincing stakeholders to invest in circular initiatives can be difficult, particularly when the benefits are long-term and not immediately visible.

To overcome these challenges, businesses need to focus on long-term value creation. While the upfront costs may be higher, the savings from reduced resource use, [carbon footprint](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/carbon-management/?ref=blog), and new revenue streams can offset these investments over time. Communicating the benefits of circular practices to stakeholders will help gain their support.

# Circular Design

## What is Circular Design?

Circular design is the practice of creating durable, reusable, repairable and recyclable products that generate zero waste to support a [circular economy](/literature/topics/circular-economy "What is a Circular Economy?").

[![








](https://public-media.interaction-design.org/images/course/poster/topic-definition-circular-design.1683640633.jpg?tr=w-1127)](/tv/course_videos/v2/topic-definition-circular-design-1080p.mp4)

In circular design, designers consider all stages of a product's lifecycle and ensure that it is sustainable from manufacturing to disposal. Some examples of the decisions involved in circular design are:

Raw material: what is the source material, and how can we extract it sustainably?

Product specifications: is the product reusable, repairable and recyclable?

Manufacturing process: is it energy-efficient, and does it generate any waste?

Distribution and use: How much energy does it take to deliver and use the product/service? Where does this energy come from?

Disposal: Can the product be dismantled? Can we use the parts as-is in the manufacturing process again? Can we return the raw materials to the earth?

![]()

The circular economy butterfly diagram by the Ellen MacArthur Foundation illustrates how circular design minimizes waste while working with renewable and non-renewable materials.

© [Ellen MacArthur Foundation](https://ellenmacarthurfoundation.org/circular-economy-diagram), Fair Use

## The Three Principles of Circular Design

**Eliminate** waste and pollution, including toxic substances that harm the soil and water and reduce emissions. Almost every product generates waste, so we need to redesign everything. Design plays a key role here.

**Circulate** products and materials at their highest value for as long as possible. A recycled product must be energy-efficient in the recycling process. Further, recycling should not result in a substantially inferior product. For example, we can build machines to last a long time and be dismantlable so manufacturers/service providers can easily repair their parts and reuse them in new products. They should be recyclable so that we do not mine more resources from the earth.

**Regenerate** natural systems by returning natural resources to the earth. For example, we can convert food waste to compost and return it to the soil to regenerate farmlands.

Circular design sounds overwhelming. However, UX pioneer [Don Norman](/literature/topics/don-norman "Who is Don Norman?") explains that we can seek inspiration from nature. Nature creates, packages, delivers and disposes off materials with zero waste.

Circular design isn't easy, but it’s possible. Here's how we can start:

**Learn** about different materials and new techniques to manufacture.

Shift from a [product mindset](/literature/topics/product-thinking "What is Product Thinking?") to a **system mindset**.

Transform business models from selling products to selling **services and experiences**.

Circular design is essential to create a circular economy and, ultimately, a healthy, life-sustaining planet. Given the precarious state of the planet, it is imperative that we adopt circular design as fast as possible and secure a better future.

The good news is that as a designer, you already have many of the skills needed to practice circular design. To know how you can leverage your design knowledge and skills and take the first steps to create circular designs, take the course **[Design For a Better World with Don Norman](https://www.interaction-design.org/courses/design-for-a-better-world-with-don-norman-course)**.

## Questions related to Circular Design

Circular design differs from traditional design by focusing on sustainability and reuse rather than a linear “take, make, dispose” model.

Here’s how they compare:

**Traditional design** follows a linear process—materials are extracted, products are created, used, and then they’re discarded as waste.

**Circular design** creates closed loops where products, components, and materials get reused, repaired, or recycled instead of being thrown away.

For example, Fairphone designs modular smartphones where users can replace parts instead of buying a new device. In contrast, traditional smartphones often become electronic waste when a single component fails.

By adopting circular design, companies reduce waste, lower costs, and create more sustainable products.

Circular design focuses on **sustainability by keeping products, materials, and resources in use for as long as possible**. It follows these key principles:

**Design for durability**—Create products that last longer and withstand wear and tear.

**Reuse and repair**—Make components easy to fix, replace, or repurpose instead of discarding them.

**Recycling and material recovery**—Use materials that can be broken down and reprocessed into new products.

**Regenerative resources**—Prioritize renewable, biodegradable, or non-toxic materials to minimize environmental impact.

**Modularity and adaptability**—Design products with interchangeable parts so users can upgrade or customize instead of replacing them entirely.

**Closed-loop systems**—Ensure materials stay in continuous cycles of use rather than ending up as waste.

For example, Patagonia designs clothing with recycled fabrics and offers repairs to extend product life. IKEA experiments with furniture rental programs to keep products in circulation.

By following these principles, designers create more sustainable, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly solutions for users—and for the planet.

Circular design keeps products and materials in use longer—thereby reducing waste and supporting a circular economy. Instead of the traditional “take, make, dispose” model, it focuses on reuse, repair, and recycling.

By designing durable and repairable products, brands reduce the need for constant replacement. Modular designs let users upgrade or replace parts instead of discarding entire products. Using recycled or biodegradable materials lowers waste, conserves resources, and helps safeguard the future of the environment.

Businesses benefit, too. Circular design cuts production costs by reusing materials and creates new revenue streams through take-back programs or refurbishing services. Brands like Patagonia repair clothing, and Fairphone makes modular smartphones to extend their lifespan.

A circular economy thrives when companies design with sustainability in mind. Fewer resources go to waste, and products stay valuable longer. This approach reduces environmental impact while driving innovation and long-term growth.

Designers create long-lasting products by focusing on **durability, repairability, and timeless design**.

They choose strong, high-quality materials that resist wear and tear. Reinforced stitching, scratch-resistant coatings, and weatherproof materials help products stay functional over time.

They also design for easy repairs. Modular components let users replace or upgrade parts instead of having to throw the whole product away. Brands like Fairphone and Patagonia make repair kits and replacement parts available to extend product life.

Timeless design plays a role, too. Avoiding trendy styles and unnecessary complexity ensures products remain useful and desirable for years—long after fads have passed.

Last, but not least, designers test products extensively. They simulate real-world conditions to ensure longevity—whether it’s stress-testing a phone’s battery or durability-testing a shoe’s sole.

By prioritizing these factors, designers reduce waste, save consumers money, and create sustainable, high-value products.

UX designers can support circular design by making sustainability easy and intuitive for users.

“Circular designers” can create interfaces that encourage repair and reuse—like clear step-by-step repair guides or product tracking apps that show when maintenance is needed. Brands like Fairphone provide user-friendly repair instructions to extend product life.

They can create seamless return and recycling experiences, too. Simple take-back programs, easy-to-find drop-off locations, and reminders for recycling make sustainable choices effortless. IKEA’s Buy Back & Resell program, for example, makes returning used furniture straightforward.

UX designers use nudges and incentives to promote circular behavior. Gamification, rewards for recycling, or clear eco-impact stats help users make greener choices.

By designing for durability, reuse, and recycling, UX designers help businesses and consumers transition to a circular economy. They make sustainability not just possible—but easy and enjoyable, too, and a great way ahead.

Circular design faces challenges that make implementation difficult for businesses and designers.

**High production costs**—Durable, repairable, and recyclable materials often cost more than their traditional “counterparts.”

**Consumer habits**—Many people still prefer convenience over sustainability, choosing cheap, disposable products instead of long-lasting ones.

**Complex supply chains**—Designing for reuse and recycling requires tracking materials across a product’s entire lifecycle, which adds complexity to the process.

**Limited infrastructure**—Many regions lack proper recycling, repair, and take-back systems, making circular products (ironically) harder to sustain.

**Company resistance**—Some businesses hesitate to adopt circular models because they rely on frequent product sales for revenue, another major “obstacle.”

Despite these challenges, brands like Fairphone, Patagonia, and IKEA show that circular design can work with the right strategies. Educating consumers, improving recycling systems, and shifting business models toward reuse and repair will help overcome these barriers and make brands more “circular” in a circular economy.

de Jesus, A., & Mendonça, S. (2018). [Lost in transition? Drivers and barriers in the eco-innovation road to the circular economy](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2017.08.001). Ecological Economics, 145, 75–89.

This paper examines the transition toward a Circular Economy (CE) by analyzing the role of Eco-Innovation (EI). The authors use a dual approach, leveraging both academic literature and policy reports, to identify key drivers and barriers in this shift. The study finds that soft factors—such as institutional, regulatory, and market-driven forces—support the transition, while hard barriers—such as technological constraints, economic lock-ins, and infrastructure limitations—impede it. By adopting a systemic innovation perspective, the authors provide an evidence-based framework for policymakers and businesses. This work is significant in understanding how transformative innovation can facilitate structural economic changes toward sustainability and resilience.

Tecchio, P., McAlister, C., Mathieux, F., & Ardente, F. (2017). [In search of standards to support circularity in product policies: A systematic approach](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.05.198). *Journal of Cleaner Production, 168*, 1533–1546.

This paper addresses a critical barrier to the Circular Economy (CE)—the lack of standardized metrics and requirements for resource efficiency in EU product policies. The authors propose a systematic framework to identify standardization needs, ensuring material efficiency requirements are effectively integrated into policy. By categorizing material efficiency into key policy goals—embodied impact reduction, lifetime extension, and residual waste reduction—the study provides a structured approach to sustainable engineering. The framework, tested on electronic displays and washing machines, highlights the role of industry, policymakers, and researchers in shaping CE standards. This research is essential for developing regulations that drive sustainable product design and waste minimization.

McDonough, W., & Braungart, M. (2002). *[Cradle to Cradle: Remaking the Way We Make Things](https://us.macmillan.com/books/9780865475878/cradletocradle)*. North Point Press.

This seminal work challenges traditional manufacturing processes by advocating for a circular approach where products are designed for continuous reuse, either as biological or technical nutrients. The authors propose a paradigm shift from the linear "cradle-to-grave" model to a regenerative "cradle-to-cradle" framework, influencing sustainable design and industrial ecology.

Charter, M. (Ed.). (2019). *[Designing for the Circular Economy](https://www.routledge.com/Designing-for-the-Circular-Economy/Charter/p/book/9781138081017)*. Routledge.

This comprehensive collection explores strategies and methodologies for integrating circular economy principles into product and service design. It addresses topics such as sustainable materials, business models, and design innovation, providing practical insights for professionals aiming to implement circular practices.

Regulations play a massive role in circular design by pushing companies to reduce waste, improve sustainability, and design for longevity.

**Right-to-repair laws**—Call for companies to make spare parts, repair manuals, and tools available so consumers can fix products instead of replacing them.

**Recycling mandates**—Make manufacturers use recyclable materials and take responsibility for product disposal. Many countries require electronics and packaging to be recyclable.

**Eco-labeling requirements**—Ensure transparency by requiring businesses to disclose environmental impact, helping consumers make informed choices.

**Bans on single-use plastics**—Push companies to design reusable or biodegradable alternatives.

**Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)**—Hold companies accountable for a product’s entire lifecycle, encouraging better design and material choices.

For example, the EU’s Circular Economy Action Plan drives sustainable product design across industries. Regulations like these encourage companies to adopt circular practices, benefiting both consumers and the environment.

## Circular Design

The [circular economy](/literature/topics/circular-economy "What is a Circular Economy?") requires [circular design](/literature/topics/circular-design "What is Circular Design?"). The three principles of circular design are:

**Eliminate** waste and pollution, including toxic substances that harm the soil and water and reduce emissions. Design plays a key role here. Almost every product generates waste, and so we need to redesign everything.

**Circulate** products and materials at their highest value for as long as possible. For example, we can build machines to last a long time and be dismantlable so their parts can be repaired easily and reused in new products. They should be recyclable so that no more resources are mined from the earth.

**Regenerate** natural systems by returning natural resources to the earth. For example, food waste can help regenerate farmlands.

![]()

The circular economy butterfly diagram by the Ellen MacArthur Foundation illustrates how we can minimize waste using both renewable and non-renewable materials.

© [Ellen MacArthur Foundation](https://ellenmacarthurfoundation.org/circular-economy-diagram), Fair Use

## The Take Away

Today, we have a linear economy. We collect raw materials from the earth, and we transform the raw materials into products which we sell to consumers. When the products are not working any longer or when they’re not trendy anymore, we consumers discard them as waste.

A circular economy generates zero waste. Once a component enters the cycle, it continues to be used for a long time. And once it is unusable, it can degrade naturally.

To create a circular economy, people from different disciplines must work together, think strategically about materials, and ensure they are reusable. [Sustainable design](https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/topics/sustainable-design) is paramount. Today, manufacturers combine different natural materials to create artificial materials. While the original components of these new materials degrade naturally, the artificial ones, once made, cannot be unmade. One of the biggest challenges is the conflict between circular manufacturing and [aesthetics](/literature/topics/aesthetics "What are Aesthetics in UX/UI Design?"). In an attempt to make something that's small, thin and lightweight, companies have made it impossible to reuse materials because they've combined them in ways that are difficult or even impossible to separate. This conflict between aesthetics and circularity is challenging, but with concerted action, an excellent opportunity for businesses.

Circular practices are set to become the cornerstone for businesses looking to embrace a holistic approach to sustainability and unlock new value delivery. There is much to consider when adopting a circular product model. In this article, we will cover the basics of circular product design, circular business model implementation, and how to avoid the most common pitfalls.Â

â

## Designing circular products

Designing products with circularity in mind is key to establishing more sustainable systems. With this mindset, products are created from their beginnings to have a reduced impact over the whole product lifetime. This means considering the impact of a product not just in its first use phase, but assuming it will be resold, remanufactured, or repaired to extend its lifetime. Designing for circularity requires the incorporation of new core design principles and insight into the materials used.Â

The core focus of circular product design is to **design out waste**. This means removing the unnecessary elements of products which use materials and generate waste. There is then the option to prioritise one of the following themes to extend the product lifetime: either **design for durability** or **design for repair and reuse**1. The goal of these two approaches is the same, but the paths to get there are very different.

Hereâs an example: If a product needs to be disassembled so that it can be easily repaired, then how the product is held together is a key consideration. An effective bonding agent to glue two materials together may increase durability and product lifespan, but it means that disassembly is no longer a viable option, sacrificing repair possibilities. Therefore, certain aspects of circularity will need to be prioritised based on your context. To make these decisions during the design process, having insight into material composition, life cycle assessment, and reuse value will be instrumental.Â

![Circular design principle priorities: durability vs reparability ](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6079869c783403bc845750f2/64d0d01ebb5cb02878415276_shoes%202%20V3.avif)

## New circular business model opportunitiesÂ

Delivering circular products changes the customer experience and your interactions with your customers. Depending on the design principle chosen, the new business opportunities will also be influenced. If the design for reparability principle is adopted, it lends to offer additional revenue streams in the form of service and repair offerings. Whereas if the design for durability principle is used, it brings opportunities in the form of new markets for high-quality, long-lasting products.

In general, adopting circular principles creates new value when the producer has a vested interest in preserving the product's lifetime and performance. This then opens up new revenue streams through models like product-as-a-service (PaaS) and resale models, previously not viable due to a disconnect in the value chain2.

To capitalise on the benefits that new circular business models bring, additional systems and services are required. Product returns from consumers must be enabled through, logistics, communication, and product processing operations. Alternatively, additional information must be passed to waste processors, so that they can return materials to the operators who value it the most, capturing the value usually lost at the productâs end of life (EOL).Â

[**Read more about PaaS models here**](/blogs/unlock-paas-models-with-dpps)

â

## Avoiding the common pitfalls of circular business models

Currently, it is rare to find a successful circular business model operating at scale, and there are several reasons for this3. But the good news is that [digital product passports](https://www.circularise.com/dpp) can help to mitigate one of these major barriers: the aversion to collaboration and data sharing between companies.

One of the major pitfalls for companies looking to implement circular practices is approaching the task from an isolated mindset of their business. Circularity requires an ecosystem approach, so the vast majority of companies lack the resources, knowledge, and capital to establish circular material flows alone.Â

Digital product passports act as a conduit to connect value chains together, regardless of size or complexity. The process of implementing digital product passports forces communication and collaboration, and once set up, they allow for reliable data sharing across supply chain tiers.Â

![Use templates such as the circular collaboration canvas to plan a circular initiative.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6079869c783403bc845750f2/64ccf8210e678e6a1c453ecf_UxVCUbOpAj0i-cd9i-UZvPLn9j5Cqq6Fbk3KSDjLxy90PuKb3G-E299Vlnhcy94Djp_bp5llVjL5aYLjVcoNUYTGRvDxbymEzsLUgb_aNLYSrKKRp1DGlD7qoMbJ2pDFEuOLI6aF93N1zMJ42xLayzE.avif)

However, even when a company accepts the need for collaboration to achieve circularity, concerns around data confidentiality and intellectual property can block progress. [Selective data sharing](https://www.circularise.com/feature/selective-data-sharing) can alleviate these concerns, enabling companies to share the necessary insights into their products and processes with the only parties that require this information, while keeping their confidential data secure.Â

As creating material loops becomes easier, the price gap between virgin and recovered materials will narrow, making the business case for circularity clearer. Overcoming these barriers will support the scaled adoption of more circular practices.Â

[**For help building the business case for digital product passports, take a look at this article**](/resource/webinar-go-circular-dpp)

â

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the transition to circular business models is a complex yet rewarding journey. It requires a shift in mindset, starting from the design phase, where products are created with durability, reparability, traceability, and recyclability in mind. The transformation extends to the business model itself, opening up new revenue streams and customer experiences. However, the path to circularity is not without its challenges.

Companies must overcome common pitfalls, such as the reluctance to collaborate, share data, and potentially risk data confidentiality. Digital product passports can play a pivotal role in mitigating these barriers, fostering communication, collaboration, and secure data sharing across the supply chain.

As the process of creating material loops becomes more streamlined and the cost gap between virgin and recovered materials narrows, the business case for circularity will become increasingly compelling. Overcoming these hurdles will pave the way for the widespread adoption of circular practices, propelling businesses towards a more sustainable and resilient future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Products should be designed to last longer, using high-quality materials and construction methods. This reduces resource depletion and enhances customer satisfaction by minimizing replacements.
Designs must allow easy access to components for repairs. Standardized parts and clear manuals extend product lifespans, fostering sustainability by reducing waste and encouraging user empowerment.
Products should be made from materials that can be easily recycled. This ensures that, at end-of-life, materials can be reused, promoting a closed-loop system that minimizes environmental impact.

Utilize lifecycle assessment tools to evaluate environmental impacts at every stage. This analysis helps identify improvement opportunities, ensuring products are sustainably designed from inception to disposal.
